div: The AI Assistant lets you work with InterSpec by typing questions and instructions in plain language. A large-language-model (LLM) reads your request together with context about the currently displayed spectrum, and then drives InterSpec's own tools on your behalf — fitting peaks, identifying nuclides, calculating decays, adjusting energy calibration, running activity/shielding and isotopic analyses, and explaining what it finds.

The assistant is opened as a tab (or window) titled "LLM Assistant". The large area shows the conversation, and you type into the input field at the bottom. Results, tool calls, and the assistant's reasoning appear in the conversation as you go.

div.p: Before you can chat, the assistant needs an LLM provider, a model, and (for most providers) an API key. If nothing is configured yet, the tool shows a "Configure LLM Provider" button; you can also open the settings at any time from the menu in the upper-right corner of the tool ("Configure LLM Provider…").

In the settings window you choose the provider (for example a hosted API endpoint or a locally running model), enter the API endpoint and bearer token/key, and pick the model name to use. Configuration is validated, and warnings are shown if, for example, the endpoint or key looks unset. Your settings are saved so you do not have to re-enter them each session.

Type a question or instruction and press Enter (or the Send button). You can refer to the spectrum you have loaded and to peaks you have already fit — the assistant is given that context automatically. Some examples of what you can ask:

ul.li: "What nuclides could be producing the peaks in this spectrum?"
"Fit the peak near 662 keV and tell me its net area."
"Is the 1460 keV peak consistent with K-40 background?"
"Check the energy calibration against the Th-232 and K-40 lines."
"Estimate the U-235 enrichment from these peaks."
"What is the activity of Cs-137, assuming 5 cm of steel shielding?"

div.p: The assistant works best when the peaks of interest have been fit — it can fit peaks for you, but you can often get better results on difficult regions by fitting them yourself first. It may take several tool calls to answer a single question; you will see each step appear in the conversation.
The menu icon in the upper-right corner of the tool provides conversation-level actions:

table.style: text-align: left; border: 1px solid black; border-collapse: collapse;

tr.td: Save the full conversation (your messages, the assistant's replies, and the tool calls it made) to a JSON file.
Discard the current conversation and start fresh.
Summarize older parts of the conversation to reduce how much context is sent to the model, while keeping the important results. Useful for long sessions.
Re-read the configuration from disk and start a new session with it.

div.p: When you send a message, your prompt is sent — along with context describing the displayed spectrum and its fit peaks — to the LLM provider you configured. Anything the assistant needs to answer (peak energies, areas, nuclide candidates, and similar analysis data) may be transmitted to that provider. Do not use a provider or model that is not approved for the sensitivity of the data you are analyzing. If you need everything to stay local, configure a locally hosted model rather than a remote hosted API.
The assistant is a tool to accelerate analysis, not a substitute for expert judgment. Always review its suggested identifications and quantitative results before relying on them.
